title: Emergency Care: Foreign Object Stuck in Cat's Eye
meta_desc: Suspect something is in your cat's eye? Learn why this is a high-urgency veterinary emergency and how to handle it safely.
content_html:
TL;DR: A foreign object in your cat’s eye is a high-priority emergency that requires immediate veterinary attention to prevent permanent damage or vision loss.

What constitutes a foreign object in my cat’s eye?

A foreign object refers to any external material—such as a grass seed, splinter, dust particle, or hair—that becomes lodged on the surface of the eye (cornea) or trapped under the eyelids. These objects cause significant irritation, inflammation, and potential trauma to the delicate ocular tissues.

Is a foreign object in my cat's eye considered a veterinary emergency?

  • Urgency Level: High. Any suspected foreign object in the eye is considered a veterinary emergency.
  • Ocular injuries can progress rapidly; delay in treatment can lead to corneal ulcers, secondary infections, or permanent vision loss.
  • If your cat is squinting, pawing at their eye, or showing signs of distress, seek immediate professional care.

How does sending a photo help the emergency vet triage my cat?

  • Providing a clear, well-lit photo allows the triage team to assess the location of the object and the degree of inflammation before you arrive.
  • A photo helps the clinic prioritize your case and determine the level of urgency.
  • This information allows staff to prepare the necessary ophthalmic tools for immediate extraction and stabilization.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Corneal foreign bodies are seen in cats and can consist of organic material, sand, metal, or glass. Presenting signs include blepharospasm, tearing, and secondary iridocyclitis characterized by aqueous flare, miosis, iridal swelling, ocular hypotony, and possible hypopyon. Diagnosis is made by ophthalmic examination to reveal the foreign body on the conjunctival surface, in the posterior third eyelid fornix, or on or in the cornea. Foreign bodies that adhere to the ocular surfaces are usually removed under topical anesthesia. Postoperative therapy often includes topical antibiotics and corticosteroids, as well as systemic antibiotics and NSAIDs. The lid must be protected from self-trauma with an Elizabethan collar, and a temporary tarsorrhaphy may be necessary to protect the cornea if the blink response is impaired.

Chapter: Ophthalmology, Emergency

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1697)

faqs.question: Should I try to remove the object myself?
faqs.answer: No. Never attempt to remove an object from your cat's eye at home. Doing so can cause the object to penetrate deeper or lead to a rupture of the eyeball. Keep your cat from pawing at the eye and head straight to a vet.

faqs.question: What are the signs of a foreign body in the eye?
faqs.answer: Common symptoms include excessive blinking or squinting (blepharospasm), clear or colored discharge, redness, cloudiness on the eye surface, and the cat repeatedly rubbing its face against furniture or with its paws.

faqs.question: What should I do while traveling to the vet?
faqs.answer: If you have an Elizabethan collar (cone), place it on your cat immediately to prevent further self-trauma. Keep the carrier dark and quiet to help reduce your cat's stress and minimize eye movement.
